item.title: What have the Mughals ever done for us?
item.description: How India’s greatest Muslim empire built its most powerful Hindu party

item.title: Why your AI assistant is suddenly selling to you
item.description: Sponsored chats are changing the way digital advertising works

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: Of God and MAGA
item.description: Charlotte Howard, our executive editor and New York bureau chief, unpacks the blurring of church and state among Donald Trump’s circle

item.title: Ukraine Has a Plan to Build Back Better
item.description: The war-torn country wants to reconstruct in a way that is environmentally, socially, and geopolitically more sustainable.

item.title: Iran Says Strait of Hormuz Is ‘Completely Open’
item.description: But it’s unclear whether the strategic waterway is really open without conditions.

item.title: Hormuz is (apparently) unblocked. Energy markets remain a mess
item.description: Mines, mistrust and missing ships will keep markets tight for months

item.title: Order Without Order
item.description: Our fixation with defining the emerging global order hides the true complexity of our neo-medieval moment.

item.title: What in the World?
item.description: Test yourself on the week of April 11: Hungary votes, Trump insults the pope, and the war in Sudan enters its fourth year.

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: The Mythos moment
item.description: An exclusive look at how we designed our cover

item.title: Did London’s Dirty Money Really Kill a Teenage Fantasist?
item.description: Patrick Radden Keefe’s “London Falling” is a mystery that turns into a tragedy.

item.title: The Iran War Comes for the ‘King of Chemicals’
item.description: The conflict is wreaking havoc on an obscure sector that is more important than you’d think.

item.title: Is bone broth good for you?
item.description: Or is it just tasty?

item.title: South American Crime Groups Are Going for Gold
item.description: Why exports of illegally sourced gold are gaining on drugs in funding the continent’s illicit economy.

item.title: South Korea’s Renewed Strategic Pivot to India
item.description: For the past decade, India-South Korea interactions have been limited. President Lee’s upcoming visit is set to change that.

item.title: The Deepening Deterioration of Public Sentiment Between Japan and China
item.description: What do Japanese and Chinese think about each other’s countries? Let’s look at the numbers.

item.title: BRICS: Can India Lead a Bloc Without a Cause?
item.description: BRICS appears to be more riddled with internal conflicts than most multilateral groups.

item.title: The World Needs an Oil Buyers’ Club
item.description: As the world is plunged into another energy crisis, market allocation is leading to grossly unjust outcomes, as the rich outbid the poor. A multilateral oil buyers' club is urgently needed to defend a price ceiling in global oil markets and allocate resources in a way that meets people’s essential needs and minimizes the economic fallout.

item.title: A New Security Architecture for the Middle East
item.description: The tense negotiations between the United States and Iran have exposed the limits of bilateral diplomacy. With the crisis fueled by overlapping, interconnected conflicts, the only viable path forward is a broader regional framework that addresses the Strait of Hormuz, nuclear proliferation, Palestinian statehood, and proxy warfare.

item.title: Japan’s Constitutional Theater: Revising Article 9 Would Be a Mistake
item.description: A constitutional revision solves the wrong problem – and creates new complications.

item.title: The Geopolitical Importance of India’s Shrinking ‘Red Corridor’
item.description: India has turned the corner in its battle against Naxalism – and effectively governing its own margins is an essential step toward global power status.

item.title: China Was Once Buying Up Sri Lankan Ports. Now It’s India’s Turn.
item.description: The Indian Ocean has no shortage of distressed strategic assets: financially stressed yards, ports, and logistics infrastructure in small states that cannot sustain them independently.

item.title: Move Over, Hungary: Spain Is China’s New Best Friend in the EU
item.description: With Viktor Orban’s election loss, Pedro Sanchez is now Beijing’s most useful European leader.

item.title: Fossil-Fuel Investments Are a Fiduciary Risk
item.description: The Iran war has reminded everyone, but especially Africans, of the structural instability of fossil-fuel prices. For African trustees, directors, asset managers, and other fiduciaries, the question is not whether capital should reposition, but whether institutions will act before events compel them to do so.

item.title: Japan’s Takaichi to Forge Closer Cooperation With Australia in Rare Earths
item.description: In an era defined by geopolitical fragmentation, the Australia-Japan partnership shows how middle powers can cooperate to mitigate risk and enhance resilience.

item.title: To Strengthen Climate Resilience, Focus on Social Protection
item.description: The international community is increasingly trying to distinguish between climate, development, and humanitarian finance—as if they can be neatly compartmentalized. But this siloed approach overlooks how social-protection programs providing cash transfers to vulnerable households can strengthen resilience to climate shocks.

item.title: A lasting Lebanese peace still looks a long way off
item.description: Donald Trump has enforced a ceasefire, but that is just a start

item.title: Europe’s Digital Decade Is in Disarray
item.description: Under the banner of the European Union’s Digital Decade agenda, Europe is investing in digitalization to protect its industries from shocks like the one currently emanating from the Middle East. But if EU leaders think their current program is sufficient, they are in for a rude awakening.

item.title: A European Populist Defends an American Pope
item.description: Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni's response to Donald Trump's attacks on Pope Leo XIV may strain her relationship with the White House, but it is likely to help her domestically. Remarkably, this outcome materialized in a staunch, if unwitting, defense of European values.

item.title: The Origins and Fate of Digital Sovereignty
item.description: Among the world’s major economies, only China and Russia have managed to build digital industries that stand apart from American platforms. Once foreign incumbents reach critical mass, local firms have little room to scale, leaving most countries without a viable path to technological autonomy.

item.title: The Uncertain Future of the China-Myanmar Economic Corridor
item.description: Despite the swearing-in of a new "civilian" government, progress on the project is likely to remain sluggish.

item.title: Vietnam’s Top Leader Concludes 4-Day State Visit to China
item.description: Meeting in Beijing, To Lam and Xi Jinping declared that they view the bilateral relationship "as a strategic choice of overarching and long-term significance.”

item.title: America and Iran’s Long Road to Peace
item.description: A grand bargain is out of reach, but a comprehensive deal is possible.

item.title: Europe Still Needs China
item.description: Washington, not Beijing, is the bigger threat.

item.title: The Iran War Is a Win for China
item.description: At a meeting with Xi next month, Trump will be on the backfoot.

item.title: The Iran War’s Impact on India and Pakistan
item.description: The economic brunt of a prolonged war would be borne by the Indian and Pakistani people, who have no part in it but no escape from its consequences.

item.title: Plugging into Reality: The ASEAN Power Grid
item.description: For decades, the Southeast Asian bloc has envisioned the creation of a region-spanning power grid. Is the project finally set for take-off?

item.title: Australian PM Secures Fuel, Fertilizer Supplies During Visits to Malaysia and Brunei
item.description: During his quick visits to the two nations, Prime Minister Anthony Albanese and his hosts pledged to “strengthen energy supply chain resilience.”

item.title: Trump’s Awful Week
item.description: The U.S. president is picking fights in every direction, alienating key supporters and allies.

item.title: Peru’s Political Thriller
item.description: The country has had nine presidents in the last decade. Who will be next?

item.title: Pope Leo Blasts ‘Tyrants’ in Thinly Veiled Critique of Trump
item.description: The White House has lashed out at the Vatican’s latest sermons on war and peace.

item.title: Republicans Twiddle Their Thumbs on Iran as Democrats Seethe
item.description: Trump’s party is still providing cover on unauthorized war despite growing signs of misgivings.

item.title: 5 Things I Wish I Knew About Iran
item.description: In the war’s aftermath, key details about the regime remain opaque.

item.title: Ukraine’s Success Still Needs Troops More Than Robots
item.description: Kyiv is struggling with skepticism of conscription fueled by Russian propaganda.

item.title: Why eldest siblings are brainier
item.description: A new study finds that sickness may play a role

item.title: Uzbekistan Wants Nuclear Energy, But Can It Afford the Water Cost?
item.description: The country is betting on nuclear power to secure its energy future – but it is doing so in one of the most water-stressed regions in the world.

item.title: The Man Who Represents Post-Clerical Iran
item.description: The rise of Mohammad Bagher Zolghadr signals the consolidation of a new type of state.

item.title: How China’s Arctic Ambitions Inflate Russia’s Geopolitical Leverage
item.description: Russia’s law suggests that Moscow is not preparing the Northern Sea Route for international use. It may instead be profiting from a misplaced expectation.

item.title: Can the Arctic Council Survive?
item.description: With the rift over Greenland, the intergovernmental body finds itself in troubled waters again.

item.title: Trump Has Made the Case for International Law
item.description: By taking a wrecking ball to the global rules-based order, Donald Trump has thrown the entire world into chaos, reminding us why our post-World War II forebears laid the foundation for that order. The task now is to re-imagine an international legal regime that works more reliably for more people, without exception for the powerful.

item.title: Adam Smith on the US Midterms
item.description: The Wealth of Nations offers a useful lens for understanding why US President Donald Trump’s mercantilist agenda has fallen short of its own stated goals. It also points to a better path, combining competitive markets with policies that help workers and communities build skills and keep pace with economic change.

item.title: When Climate Lies Kill: Red-Tagging Indigenous Defenders in the Philippines
item.description: Climate disinformation is strategically deployed to reinforce red-tagging narratives, portraying Indigenous resistance to mining, energy, and infrastructure projects as a threat to national security.

item.title: Tumour cells use a genetic trick to become drug-resistant
item.description: The same trick could also be used against them

item.title: How to Future-Proof the Global Economy
item.description: The backlash against globalization, the slow death of the Washington Consensus, and the rapid rise of AI are fueling volatility that, if left unchecked, will lead to lower growth, higher inflation, and greater inequality. To move the economy onto a better path requires, first and foremost, abandoning our faith in outdated ideas.

item.title: Beyond the Rupture: Where Are China-Japan Relations Heading?
item.description: The evolution of Sino-Japanese relations has rarely followed a linear path – but it has always remained within clear guardrails, even during times of tension.

item.title: 5-Year Prison Terms for Atajurt Activists Who Burned Chinese Flag
item.description: After protesting Chinese state policies in Xinjiang, and after apparent pressure from Beijing, the group of Kazakhs were charged with “inciting national hatred.”

item.title: Australia’s New National Defense Strategy Feels Written for a Bygone Era
item.description: It ignores the elephant in the room: an unreliable and unruly United States.

item.title: China’s Taiwan Calculus Ahead of the Trump-Xi Summit
item.description: Beijing is not seeking a breakthrough on Taiwan at the Trump-Xi summit, but rather incremental gains that could gradually weaken Taiwan-U.S. ties.

item.title: The impending global food shock is preventable
item.description: So why won’t it be prevented?

item.title: Is the US Falling Victim to the Resource Curse?
item.description: The argument that the natural-resource curse is at work in the US rests on the assumption that rising oil and gas production will necessarily crowd out manufacturing, including renewables. But while the argument is increasingly heard, past experience in the US—as well as in Australia, Chile, Norway, and elsewhere—does not support it.

item.title: PPP’s Chief Holds Meetings in Washington While His Party Faces Ruin at Home
item.description: People Power Party leader Jang Dong-hyeok’s Washington trip reveals more about his internal survival strategy than any genuine foreign policy agenda.

item.title: China’s Definition of an ‘Evil Cult’ Is Expanding Beyond Religious Groups
item.description: “Create Abundance” is not, in any conventional sense, a religion. For that reason, its persecution signals a dangerous expansion of China’s ideological control.

item.title: A New Dataset Maps Central Asia’s Extractive Economy
item.description: An open-access dataset from the Oxus Society maps more than $118 billion in resource exports across the five Central Asian republics, offering a rare quantitative window into the region’s shifting place in Eurasian supply chains.

item.title: Only Peace Can Make a New World Order
item.description: Today’s wars are tearing down the existing global system, but they can’t replace it.

item.title: Is the Iran War America’s Suez or Its Gallipoli?
item.description: The Iran war is not America’s "Suez moment," but it may well turn out to be America’s Gallipoli moment. It was there, in 1915, that the British Empire overreached disastrously—underestimating the tenacity of a decadent regime, overestimating its own military capacity, and demonstrating stupendous strategic confusion.

item.title: Hungary’s Counter-Counterrevolution
item.description: Ordinary Hungarians have paid a steep price for Viktor Orbán’s smash-and-grab mafia state, where family and friends enriched themselves while living standards stagnated. Voters have turned the page on Orbán, and now the question is whether Hungary can turn the corner after 16 years of his "illiberal democracy."

item.title: Xinjiang’s Repression of Uyghurs Has Evolved, Not Ended
item.description: A rare insider testimony reveals how China tries to hide state violence in Xinjiang

item.description: Could workers kicked out of the Gulf find fortune in East Asia?

item.description: For now, they are still a long way behind

item.description: Beijing is working subtly to shape the regional environment ahead of the meeting – and take advantage of Washington’s focus on the Iran war.

item.description: Targeting the inner circle of leadership shows no one is safe.

item.title: How Strategic Investment Is Redefining Economic Statecraft
item.description: In a global economy defined by intertwined supply chains and cross-border capital flows, economic security cannot rely solely on national investment. Fund-of-funds vehicles can help governments bolster resilience and advance strategic objectives while protecting sensitive technologies and limiting adversarial influence.

item.description: Allowing only a privileged few companies to use them may make commercial sense

item.description: The plan was discussed even as Indian and Bangladeshi diplomats are working to reset bilateral relations.

item.title: Bangladesh at the Crossroads: Renewables or Imported Fuel Chaos
item.description: The energy crisis is hitting Bangladesh’s 177 million people hard. Experts have been urging the government to end dependence on imported coal and gas in favor of reliable renewables.

item.title: The Next UN Secretary-General Must Be a Woman
item.description: As United Nations member states prepare to elect a new secretary-general, they should break with precedent and choose a woman. This would not be a corrective gesture or a form of symbolic representation, but rather a strategic acknowledgment that leadership selection must reflect the realities of contemporary governance.

item.description: Insights from Jonathan Cheng.

item.description: “If the United States is serious about a stable and resilient Indo-Pacific, it must act to strengthen its economic relationships” with the Pacific Islands, argues U.S. Rep. Ed Case.

item.description: The conflict complicates life for the custodians of Middle Eastern oil fortunes

item.description: It’s not just about Israel: Seoul is signaling political confidence and a growing readiness to shape its own external posture separately from the United States.

item.description: The charging of the officer comes amid increased scrutiny of the deaths of Kazakh soldiers, often quite young, during peacetime.

item.title: Amid the Hormuz Crisis, Indonesia Rewrites Its Energy Playbook
item.description: Through summit diplomacy and state visits, Prabowo is widening Indonesia’s options across oil, gas, minerals, industrial inputs, and energy transition technologies.

item.title: Hungary’s New Leader Must Avoid Joe Biden’s Mistakes
item.description: If Hungary can become a model for illiberal movements elsewhere to emulate, it can also become a sandbox for experimenting with democratic renewal. America and others suffering democratic backsliding can offer lessons for Péter Magyar's new government, whose successes or failures, in turn, will generate new lessons.

item.description: From Gaza to Lebanon to Iran, Netanyahu has sought to end threats rather than manage them.

item.description: The 35-year-old is building an empire spanning railways, energy and mining. Most Kazakhs have never heard of him.

item.title: Putting Africa’s Savings to Work
item.description: Africa’s pension funds and financial institutions hold hundreds of billions of dollars in long-term savings, yet much of that capital flows into sovereign bonds because few scalable alternatives exist. The task now is to build the architecture that channels those savings into productive investments.

item.title: The Great Hungarian Reset
item.description: Now that he has ousted Viktor Orbán, incoming Hungarian Prime Minister Péter Magyar inherits an economy burdened by a legacy of centralized mismanagement and systemic corruption. To address it, he will need a focused and pragmatic economic program.

item.title: The Hormuz Crisis and the Fate of the Global South
item.description: If the shock from the closure of the Strait of Hormuz has revealed economic vulnerabilities, it has also illuminated stark differences in how countries absorb energy-price turbulence. Those that have invested in more resilient clean energy sources are faring better and offering lessons for everyone else.

item.title: Japan’s PM Faces Gas Tax Headwinds When She Flies to Australia
item.description: As Australia’s biggest gas customer, Japan is alarmed by growing calls for a new tax on gas exports. Elsewhere in Asia, leaders recognize renewables can help break reliance on volatile fossil fuel supply chains.

item.description: China is not moving from coal to renewables in a linear transition. Instead, it is attempting to expand both simultaneously.

item.description: Despite a clear "yes" vote in the referendum, the BNP government is dragging its feet, prompting the opposition to protest in parliament and the streets.

item.title: Why Development Doesn’t Prevent War
item.description: Violent conflicts have reached levels not seen since World War II, even as global poverty has fallen to historic lows, challenging long-held assumptions about the relationship between development and peace. This outcome calls for a reassessment of the theory of change that underpins development aid.

item.title: Russians Go Home
item.description: At a time when illiberalism has presented itself as the wave of the future, Hungarians just voted overwhelmingly to reverse course. But unlike the country’s liberation in 1989, this week’s electoral result represents only the beginning of a longer process, the outcome of which will remain uncertain.

item.title: Indian Navy Needs More Stringent Planning

While that rules out confident prediction, the global connectedness of these risks means that what comes next is certain to reverberate worldwide.
